title: In my view\br /in mái viu/
definition: Formal way to express your opinion. Less direct than 'I think'. \br \br Use in professional meetings, presentations, and emails.
example1: In my view, we should focus on customer retention first. \br In my view, the data supports a different conclusion. \br In my view, this approach is too risky.
example3: Alternatives: 'From my perspective', 'It seems to me that'.

title: As far as I'm concerned\br /az far az áim konsérnd/
definition: States a firm personal perspective on a specific matter. Often used to draw a boundary or express a strong opinion. \br \br Slightly emphatic – use when you want to be clear where you stand.
example1: As far as I'm concerned, the budget is non‑negotiable. \br As far as I'm concerned, we've waited long enough. \br As far as I'm concerned, this project is a priority.
example3: Sounds a bit defensive if overused. Reserve for when you need to assert a clear position.

title: I'd say\br /áid sêi/
definition: Slightly less formal way to offer a suggestion or observation. Often used when expressing a tentative opinion or estimate. \br \br Common in British and American business speech.
example1: I'd say we're about halfway through the timeline. \br I'd say it's time to reconsider our strategy. \br I'd say that's a fair point, but we need more data.
example3: Softer than 'I think' – good for diffusing tension.

title: What are your thoughts?\br /uót ar ior fóts/
definition: Formal and professional way to ask for someone's opinion. Shows respect and invites open discussion. \br \br Excellent for encouraging participation from quieter team members.
example1: What are your thoughts on the new proposal, Sarah? \br We've heard the first two options. What are your thoughts? \br Before we decide, what are your thoughts on the risk factors?
example3: Follow up with silence – let them answer without rushing.

title: Do you have any input?\br /dú iu hév êni íinput/
definition: Asking for ideas, feedback, or thoughts from a person or the group. Very direct but polite. \br \br Useful when you know someone has expertise in the area.
example1: Do you have any input on the marketing timeline? \br We've covered most items. Do you have any input before we move on? \br I've shared my view – do you have any input, John?
example3: Avoid using it as a rhetorical question. Wait for an answer.

title: I'd like to hear from you, [Name]\br /áid láik tchi híar from iu/
definition: Direct and polite way to invite a specific person to speak. Often used by a meeting leader to bring someone into the conversation. \br \br Shows you value that person's opinion.
example1: I'd like to hear from you, Maria. What's your perspective? \br We haven't heard from the engineering team – I'd like to hear from you, Tom. \br I'd like to hear from you all. Please share your thoughts.
example3: Use this instead of pointing or saying 'you, speak' – much more professional.

title: I couldn't agree more\br /ái cúdent agrí mór/
definition: Shows very strong agreement. Emphatic but still professional. \br \br Use when you fully support what was just said.
example1: When she said we need faster response times, I couldn't agree more. \br I couldn't agree more with your analysis of the market. \br He suggested a second review? I couldn't agree more.
example3: Avoid overusing – it loses impact if said too often.

title: That makes perfect sense\br /dét mêiks pêrfekt sénts/
definition: Validates that the previous point was logical and clear. Shows you understand and accept the reasoning. \br \br Useful for building consensus.
example1: That makes perfect sense. I support the proposal. \br Now that you've explained it, that makes perfect sense. \br Considering the budget constraints, that makes perfect sense.
example3: Can be followed by 'but' if you partially agree – but use carefully.

title: I agree to an extent\br /ái agrí tu an ikstént/
definition: Used for partial agreement. You agree up to a certain limit, but have reservations. \br \br Diplomatic and avoids absolute disagreement.
example1: I agree to an extent that social media is important, but our main channel is email. \br I agree to an extent – the numbers look good, but the timing is off. \br She made good points, and I agree to an extent. However...
example3: Always follow with 'however' or 'but' to explain your reservation.

title: I'm not sure I agree\br /áim nót xúr ái agrí/
definition: A soft, polite way to disagree even if you are certain you don't. Reduces defensiveness. \br \br Excellent for maintaining relationships while expressing a different view.
example1: I'm not sure I agree that cost is the only factor. \br I'm not sure I agree with that interpretation of the data. \br You make good points, but I'm not sure I agree with the conclusion.
example3: Use a calm, neutral tone. Don't sound hesitant – just polite.

title: I have a different point of view\br /ái hév a dífrent póint ov viu/
definition: Suggests you have an alternative perspective without being confrontational. Focuses on the idea, not the person. \br \br Very diplomatic.
example1: I have a different point of view on how to allocate the budget. \br Thanks for sharing. I have a different point of view based on my experience. \br I have a different point of view, but I'd like to hear what others think first.
example3: State your alternative clearly after this phrase. Don't just leave it hanging.

title: To be blunt, I completely disagree\br /tu bí blãnt, ái complítli disagrí/
definition: Used when you need to be very direct and straight to the point. High impact – use sparingly and only when necessary. \br \br May cause friction, so be sure the situation calls for it.
example1: To be blunt, I completely disagree with that strategy. \br To be blunt, I completely disagree that we should delay the launch. \br I respect your opinion, but to be blunt, I completely disagree.
example3: Only use with people who appreciate directness. Otherwise, choose a softer phrase.

title: Could you help me understand?\br /cúd iu hélp mi andersténd/
definition: Invites the speaker to elaborate or explain a point further without sounding accusatory. Very polite and curious. \br \br Excellent for clarifying without putting anyone on the defensive.
example1: Could you help me understand how you arrived at that number? \br I'm not following the logic. Could you help me understand? \br Could you help me understand the trade‑offs here?
example3: Tone matters – use a genuine, curious tone, not sarcastic.

title: I'm sorry, I didn't quite follow that\br /áim sári, ái dídent kuáit fálou dét/
definition: A polite way to say you didn't understand what was just said. Takes responsibility ('I didn't follow') rather than blaming the speaker. \br \br Very useful in technical or fast‑paced meetings.
example1: I'm sorry, I didn't quite follow that last part. Could you repeat it? \br I'm sorry, I didn't quite follow your explanation of the timeline. \br I missed the transition – I'm sorry, I didn't quite follow that.
example3: Never say 'you're not making sense'. This phrase is much better.

title: Correct me if I'm wrong, but...\br /corékt mi if áim rông, bãt/
definition: Shows humility while questioning a point or stating a potentially incorrect fact. Lowers the stakes. \br \br Great for challenging assumptions without offending.
example1: Correct me if I'm wrong, but isn't the deadline next Friday? \br Correct me if I'm wrong, but that data seems to contradict our earlier findings. \br Correct me if I'm wrong, but we approved this budget last quarter.
example3: Pause after saying it to let the other person confirm or correct you.

title: May I add something, [Name]?\br /mêi ái éd sãmthing/
definition: A polite way to contribute to the conversation while someone else is speaking. Shows respect for the current speaker. \br \br Formal and courteous.
example1: May I add something, Sarah? I think we also need to consider the legal side. \br Before we move on, may I add something? \br May I add something to what John just said?
example3: Wait for acknowledgement before speaking. Don't just interrupt.

title: Can I jump in here quickly?\br /kén ái djãmp in híar kuíkli/
definition: An informal way to interrupt and add a quick point. Less formal than 'May I add something?' but still polite. \br \br Good for fast‑paced meetings where you need to interject briefly.
example1: Can I jump in here quickly? I just want to clarify one point. \br Sorry to interrupt, but can I jump in here quickly? \br Can I jump in here quickly – we're running over time.
example3: Keep your point very brief. If it needs more time, ask to put it on the agenda.

title: If I could just finish my point\br /if ái cúd djãst fínix mái póint/
definition: Use this when someone else interrupts you and you want to finish your thought. Firm but polite. \br \br Assertive without being aggressive.
example1: If I could just finish my point, then I'm happy to hear your perspective. \br I was about to say the same thing – if I could just finish my point. \br Let me finish, please. If I could just finish my point.
example3: Say it calmly. Don't raise your voice. Maintain eye contact.

title: Let me finish my thought, then I'd love to hear your perspective\br /lét mi fínix mái fót, dén áid lóv tchi híar ior perspéktiv/
definition: A formal way to maintain the floor while acknowledging the interrupter. Shows you respect their input but need to complete your idea first. \br \br Very polished and professional.
example1: I hear you, but let me finish my thought, then I'd love to hear your perspective. \br Let me finish my thought, then I'd love to hear your perspective on the budget. \br I appreciate the input – let me finish my thought, then I'd love to hear yours.
example3: Say it with a smile to keep the tone friendly.

title: I suggest\br / I propose\br /ái sugdjést/\br /ái propôus/
definition: Standard ways to offer a plan. 'Propose' is more formal and often used in written proposals or high‑level meetings. \br \br Strong and clear.
example1: I suggest we break into smaller groups. \br I propose a phased approach: first research, then pilot, then rollout. \br I suggest we revisit this after lunch when everyone is fresh.
example3: Use 'I suggest' for everyday meetings, 'I propose' for formal or high‑stakes situations.

title: Here's something we can consider\br /hírz sãmthing ui kén konsíder/
definition: Piques curiosity and grabs attention before sharing an idea. Soft opener that doesn't force agreement. \br \br Very collaborative and low‑pressure.
example1: Here's something we can consider: what if we outsourced customer support? \br Here's something we can consider – moving the deadline by one week. \br I know we've discussed options, but here's something we can consider.
example3: Pause after saying it to build anticipation.

title: How about\br / What about...?\br /ráu abáut/\br /uót abáut/
definition: Informal ways to introduce an alternative or a suggestion. Good for brainstorming sessions or smaller meetings. \br \br Casual but effective.
example1: How about we test the concept with a focus group first? \br What about using a different vendor for the printing? \br How about we table this until the next meeting and focus on urgent items?
example3: Avoid in very formal board meetings. Use 'I suggest' instead.

title: Good question, let me think about that for a second\br /gúd quéstchen, lét mi tínk abáut dét for a sékend/
definition: Validates the question while giving your brain time to process. Shows you take the question seriously. \br \br Honest and disarming.
example1: Good question, let me think about that for a second. \br That's a great point – good question, let me think about that for a second. \br Good question, let me think about that for a second. I'll come back to you.
example3: Use a thoughtful pause. Don't rush to answer. It shows confidence.

title: I'd like to give that a little more thought\br /áid láik tchi gív dét a líru mór fót/
definition: Signals you need more time before giving a definitive answer. Avoids making a premature decision. \br \br Professional and responsible.
example1: That's an interesting proposal. I'd like to give that a little more thought. \br I'd like to give that a little more thought before I commit. \br Let me take that away – I'd like to give that a little more thought.
example3: Follow up with a timeline: 'Can I get back to you tomorrow?'

title: I'd like to revisit that after I've had a chance to review\br /áid láik tchi rivíxit dét áftar áiv héd a tchéns tchi rivíu/
definition: Used to defer a discussion until you have looked at the necessary data or information. Shows thoroughness. \br \br Very professional and responsible.
example1: I'd like to revisit that after I've had a chance to review the latest sales figures. \br I'm not comfortable deciding now – I'd like to revisit that after I've had a chance to review. \br Let's note it and I'd like to revisit that after I've had a chance to review the contract.
example3: Always specify what you need to review and when you will come back to it.

title: Let's circle back on that\br /lêts sêrkul bék on dét/
definition: Means to return to a topic later. Very common in modern business English. \br \br Useful for parking a topic when time is short.
example1: We're out of time – let's circle back on that tomorrow. \br I don't have the data now. Let's circle back on that after lunch. \br Let's circle back on that when the full report is ready.
example3: Set a specific time to circle back, otherwise it may never happen.

title: Touch base\br /tãtch bêis/
definition: To make brief contact with someone to update or check in. Very common in American business. \br \br Informal but widely accepted.
example1: I'll touch base with the marketing team and get back to you. \br Let's touch base next week to see how the project is going. \br Can we touch base quickly before the client call?
example3: Use 'touch base with someone' – never 'touch base to someone'.

title: Run it up the flagpole\br /rãn it ãp dja flégpôul/
definition: Idiom meaning to test an idea or proposal to see if it gains support. Very informal, used in creative industries. \br \br Means 'let's see how people react'.
example1: Let's run it up the flagpole and see if anyone salutes. \br I like that concept – let's run it up the flagpole at the next meeting. \br We should run it up the flagpole before investing too much time.
example3: Can sound dated. Use with colleagues who appreciate playful language.

title: Table this item\br /têibu dxis áitem/
definition: To postpone discussion of a topic to a future meeting. Used mainly in American English. \br \br Standard meeting management phrase.
example1: Let's table this item until we have the budget numbers. \br I move to table this discussion until the next meeting. \br We're getting stuck. Let's table this item and come back to it.
example3: In British English, 'table' means the opposite (to bring forward). Know your audience.

title: Action item\br /ákxen áitem/
definition: A specific task assigned to someone during a meeting. Essential for follow‑up. \br \br Used in almost every professional meeting.
example1: I'll send an email with all the action items by tomorrow. \br Your action item is to contact IT about the server issue. \br Let's review action items from last meeting before we move on.
example3: Always assign a due date to every action item.
